birth.notes: EH gives July 3, 1847 Hartford CT (father Henry cashier of Phoenix Bank Hartford at this date); FindAGrave inconsistently shows July 8, 1847 Greenfield MA. EH preferred.

education.notes: Williston Seminary at Easthampton MA prepared him for Williams College.

career.notes: Three years in the Paris office of Bolles Brothers investment bankers after Williams 1871 graduation.
Treasurer of the Jessup & Lafflin Paper Company of Westfield MA.
Nearly fifty-five years with Kidder, Peabody & Company Boston banking firm — per his December 8, 1932 New York Times obituary. Close friend of literary critic Gamaliel Bradford.

narrative_blocks.body: After Williams College 1871 and three years in the Paris office of Bolles Brothers (1872-1875), he spent nearly fifty-five years with Kidder, Peabody & Company — the Boston banking firm whose name would later become synonymous with twentieth-century American investment banking. His December 1932 New York Times obituary called him a 'close friend of the late Gamaliel Bradford.'

Educated at Westfield High School, then Williston Seminary at Easthampton MA, then Williams College in the Berkshires graduating 1871. Also treasurer of the Jessup & Lafflin Paper Company of Westfield alongside his banking work — the kind of manufacturing-finance combination characteristic of late 19th-c New England merchant capital.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost Ellen and two of three children young
narrative_blocks.body: Married Ellen Jane Goodwin June 20, 1878 Pittsfield MA. She died July 31, 1901 Wellesley MA of nephritis age 44. Their daughter Sybil Hale died 1906 age 26; daughter Sarah Hale had died 1885 age 2 of uremic poisoning.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost Ellen and two of three children young
narrative_blocks.body: Married Ellen Jane Goodwin June 20, 1878 Pittsfield MA. She died July 31, 1901 Wellesley MA of nephritis age 44. Their daughter Sybil Hale died 1906 age 26; daughter Sarah Hale had died 1885 age 2 of uremic poisoning.

death.cause: Nephritis (uraemia) with pulmonary phthisis (per death record 'Nephritis Cursomia and Pulmonary Phithisis')

parents.father_research_notes: Charles A. Goodwin of Pittsfield MA. Per EH 1909 #4365: 'Her father was brevetted Lieutenant during the war of the Rebellion and was killed in the battle of the Wilderness' — Civil War casualty May 1864.

narrative_blocks.header: Father killed at Battle of the Wilderness 1864
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Charles A. Goodwin of Pittsfield MA was brevetted Lieutenant during the Civil War and killed at the Battle of the Wilderness — Union Army's bloodiest May 1864 engagement in the Virginia Overland Campaign. She was seven years old.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her great-grandmother Sybella Hale Hall (X02861, 1797-1884) of Greenfield MA — mother of Sarah Hall Hooker (I01127, Sybil's grandmother). The 'Sybil' form is a simplified spelling of the great-grandmother's 'Sybella.'

birth.notes: EH gives Oct 16, 1881; FindAGrave gives April 16, 1881 Boston — discrepancy. EH preferred.

narrative_blocks.header: Only surviving child of Kidder-Peabody banker Charles
narrative_blocks.body: Sole surviving child of Charles Hall Hooker (Williams 1871, 55-year Kidder-Peabody banker) and Ellen Jane Goodwin — his elder sister Sybil Hale died 1906 age 26, younger sister Sarah Hale died 1885 age 2 of uremic poisoning. Married Honore Frances O'Dea ('Frances Hooker') December 15, 1923 at Suffolk MA age 42; survived his father by 10 years.

bio.extra_names: Honore Frances O'Dea (given) — Frances Hooker (married name; how she was known)

burial.plot_notes: Headstone inscription: 'Daughter of Charles H. & Ellen J. Hooker.'

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her grandmother Sarah Hall Hooker (I01127, 1819-1900) who herself carried the maternal Hale-family name from great-grandmother Sybella Hale Hall (X02861) of Greenfield MA. The 'Hale' middle name carried the Westhampton matriarchal identity three generations forward.

birth.notes: FindAGrave gives Westfield (family moved 1819 per EH H01410); EH 1909 has 'West hampton' likely a typo or family-origin reference. Using Westfield as more consistent with timeline.

death.cause: Mitral regurgitation of the heart (per death record)

career.organization: Phoenix Bank Hartford CT; Great Barrington bank; Westfield Bank
career.notes: Started as clerk in Phoenix Bank Hartford CT 1836 age 15; remained connected with that bank until 1847. Cashier of bank at Great Barrington MA 1847. Cashier of Westfield Bank, Westfield MA from 1851.

cross_connections.display_label: Mother of his wife Sarah Hall; his aunt through the Hale family
His uncle through the Hale family; married two Hooker women

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin Sarah Hall, 1844
narrative_blocks.body: Married Sarah Hall (1819-1898) of Greenfield MA on October 10, 1844. Sarah's mother Sibylla Hale Hall was his own mother Octavia's sister — both daughters of Rev Enoch Hale of Westhampton — making the bride and groom first cousins and the marriage a second Hale-Hooker knot in the same generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Bank clerk at fifteen, Westfield Bank cashier
narrative_blocks.body: Became a clerk in the Phoenix Bank at Hartford CT in 1836 at age fifteen — staying eleven years before moving to Great Barrington MA as bank cashier in 1847. From 1851 he was cashier of the Westfield Bank in Westfield MA, where he remained until his death June 13, 1896 age 75.

death.notes: FindAGrave records May 23, 1900 Wellesley MA age 80. EH 1909 had given Oct 12, 1898 Cambridgeport MA — discrepancy; using FindAGrave as more reliable for vital records.

parents.father_research_notes: Dr. William Hooker (H00529, 1766-?), physician + trader at West Hampton MA; born Northampton MA Nov 26, 1766; son of Rev John and Sarah Worthington Hooker of Northampton. Per EH 1909 #529: 'William Hooker (John* John* John,3 Samuel,2 Thomas1)...m. 1798 Anna Thompson, daughter of Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT. She died at West Hampton MA. He married No. 2, Widow of Ensign Hartland.'
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Thompson Hooker not yet in tree.

career.location: West Hampton, Norwich, and Westfield Massachusetts
career.notes: Merchant at West Hampton MA, then Norwich MA. In 1819 removed family to Westfield MA, where they remained.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved family from Norwich Massachusetts to Westfield, 1819
narrative_blocks.body: Merchant at West Hampton MA before relocating to Norwich MA where his two eldest children Lucy Ashmun and William Throop were born. In 1819 he moved the family to Westfield MA, where son Edward was born 1822 and where William and his wife Octavia would both be buried at Pine Hill Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Lucy Ashmun died at Rushville Illinois, 1839
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest child Lucy Ashmun died age 26 at Rushville IL April 17, 1839 — only seventeen months after her Massachusetts marriage to Dr Joseph Washburn Clark and five weeks after the birth of their infant son who himself died in May. The Illinois frontier had taken his daughter, son-in-law's first family, and grandson all within a year.

parents.mother_research_notes: Octavia Throop Hale (1754-1839) of Bozrah CT, died Westhampton MA aged 85. Not yet built (deferred).

cross_connections.display_label: Uncle executed as Continental Army spy, September 1776

narrative_blocks.header: Niece of Nathan Hale the patriot martyr
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Rev Enoch Hale of Westhampton MA was elder brother of Capt Nathan Hale — the Continental Army officer hanged as spy by the British at New York September 22, 1776. Nathan died nearly twelve years before her birth; the family memory of the executed uncle shaped her own brother's name (Nathan Hale Sr 1784-1863) and reached into her children's generation through her son's cousin marriage to her sister Sybella Hale Hall's daughter Sarah.

narrative_blocks.header: Died of dementia paralytica at Northampton, 1867
narrative_blocks.body: Died December 21, 1867 at Northampton MA age 79 — eighteen months after her husband — with cause given on her death record as 'Dementile Paralysis,' the period name for dementia paralytica, the late-stage neurological syndrome of progressive cognitive and motor decline.

marriages.notes: Eleven children documented per FamilySearch: Sarah Hall (I01127, b.1819 m. Henry Hooker), John Richardson Hall (1821-1890), Charles Hall (1823-1849), Almira Hale Hall (1825-1889), William Hooker Hall (1827-1891), Mary Hall (1828), Edward Hall (1829), Isabella Hall (1832-1833 infant), Isabella Hall 2nd (1834-1853), Mary Eloise Hall (1837-1890), Capt Henry Throop Hall (1839-1897). Only Sarah Hall (I01127) built; other 10 deferred.

cross_connections.display_label: Her nephew who married her daughter Sarah Hall, 1844

narrative_blocks.header: Sister Octavia married into the Hooker line, 1811
narrative_blocks.body: Elder sister Octavia Hale married William Hooker Jr at Westhampton December 19, 1811 — eight years before Sybella's own 1818 marriage to Richardson Hall. The two sisters' lines knotted a second time when Sybella's eldest daughter Sarah married Octavia's own son Henry Hooker in October 1844, a first-cousin marriage between the two Hale-line branches.

narrative_blocks.header: Eleven children with Greenfield Massachusetts merchant
narrative_blocks.body: Married Richardson Hall of Norwich MA at Franklin County MA in 1818 and bore eleven children including Sarah Hall (1819-1900), Almira Hale Hall (1825-1889), William Hooker Hall (1827-1891), and Capt Henry Throop Hall (1839-1897). Two infants both named Isabella died in 1833 and 1853 respectively. She survived her husband by 24 years.

narrative_blocks.header: Sister wed William Hooker; daughter wed William's son
narrative_blocks.body: In October 1844, Sybella's daughter Sarah Hall married Henry Hooker — the son of Sybella's elder sister Octavia, who had herself married into the Hooker family thirty-three years earlier in 1811. Two generations of Hale women thus married into the same Westhampton Hooker household, completing a family bond begun by Nathan Hale's nieces.

INST043.name: Williams College
INST043.notes: Private liberal arts college founded 1793 with funds from the estate of Ephraim Williams, a colonist killed in the French and Indian War (1755). One of the original "Little Three" liberal arts colleges.
